TEXT MAP v2 — two ALIGNED layers, one grid frame, north-up. 1 cell ≈ 5.3m. col 0..47 = W→E, row 0..27 = N→S (row 0 = north). Cells are space-separated (one symbol = one cell).
GRID REF — cell(col,row) centre = [lng, lat]:  lng = -73.9769713 + (col+0.5)*4.1727e-5;  lat = 40.7586434 - (row+0.5)*6.3097e-5
CROSS-REFERENCE: the layers share coordinates. Look up the SAME (col,row) in both layers: an equipment marker over '#' in LAYER 1 sits INSIDE that building; a cable glyph over '#' crosses that building — UNLESS that building is the cable's own source or target (a drop legitimately ENDS inside the building it serves; check CABLES source -> target).
HOW TO READ (hypothetical example, NOT from this scene): if LAYER 2 shows marker 'q' at (3,1) and LAYER 1 at (3,1) shows '#', that equipment sits INSIDE that building; '=' or '|' there means it sits on a street; ':' or '.' means open ground. The LEGEND's inside= field precomputes building containment exactly for every equipment item.
GEOMETRY vs TOPOLOGY: the grids show WHERE things are (geometry). WHO connects to whom (topology) is in the LEGEND — each closure's serves= list and the CABLES section's source -> target. For connectivity/path questions, read the LEGEND; do not trace glyphs.
THRESHOLDS: when a question states a numeric distance (e.g. 'within ~8m'), compare it against the LEGEND's measurements (d_street=, x/y meters) — grid glyphs are drawings, not distance judgments.

TEXT MAP v2 — two ALIGNED layers, one grid frame, north-up. 1 cell ≈ 5.0m. col 0..47 = W→E, row 0..27 = N→S (row 0 = north). Cells are space-separated (one symbol = one cell).
GRID REF — cell(col,row) centre = [lng, lat]:  lng = -73.9835762 + (col+0.5)*4.0635e-5;  lat = 40.7495762 - (row+0.5)*5.9378e-5
CROSS-REFERENCE: the layers share coordinates. Look up the SAME (col,row) in both layers: an equipment marker over '#' in LAYER 1 sits INSIDE that building; a cable glyph over '#' crosses that building — UNLESS that building is the cable's own source or target (a drop legitimately ENDS inside the building it serves; check CABLES source -> target).
HOW TO READ (hypothetical example, NOT from this scene): if LAYER 2 shows marker 'q' at (3,1) and LAYER 1 at (3,1) shows '#', that equipment sits INSIDE that building; '=' or '|' there means it sits on a street; ':' or '.' means open ground. The LEGEND's inside= field precomputes building containment exactly for every equipment item.
GEOMETRY vs TOPOLOGY: the grids show WHERE things are (geometry). WHO connects to whom (topology) is in the LEGEND — each closure's serves= list and the CABLES section's source -> target. For connectivity/path questions, read the LEGEND; do not trace glyphs.
THRESHOLDS: when a question states a numeric distance (e.g. 'within ~8m'), compare it against the LEGEND's measurements (d_street=, x/y meters) — grid glyphs are drawings, not distance judgments.

TEXT MAP v2 — two ALIGNED layers, one grid frame, north-up. 1 cell ≈ 4.0m. col 0..47 = W→E, row 0..27 = N→S (row 0 = north). Cells are space-separated (one symbol = one cell).
GRID REF — cell(col,row) centre = [lng, lat]:  lng = -73.9919402 + (col+0.5)*3.6204e-5;  lat = 40.7533616 - (row+0.5)*4.4839e-5
CROSS-REFERENCE: the layers share coordinates. Look up the SAME (col,row) in both layers: an equipment marker over '#' in LAYER 1 sits INSIDE that building; a cable glyph over '#' crosses that building — UNLESS that building is the cable's own source or target (a drop legitimately ENDS inside the building it serves; check CABLES source -> target).
HOW TO READ (hypothetical example, NOT from this scene): if LAYER 2 shows marker 'q' at (3,1) and LAYER 1 at (3,1) shows '#', that equipment sits INSIDE that building; '=' or '|' there means it sits on a street; ':' or '.' means open ground. The LEGEND's inside= field precomputes building containment exactly for every equipment item.
GEOMETRY vs TOPOLOGY: the grids show WHERE things are (geometry). WHO connects to whom (topology) is in the LEGEND — each closure's serves= list and the CABLES section's source -> target. For connectivity/path questions, read the LEGEND; do not trace glyphs.
THRESHOLDS: when a question states a numeric distance (e.g. 'within ~8m'), compare it against the LEGEND's measurements (d_street=, x/y meters) — grid glyphs are drawings, not distance judgments.

TEXT MAP v2 — two ALIGNED layers, one grid frame, north-up. 1 cell ≈ 2.9m. col 0..47 = W→E, row 0..27 = N→S (row 0 = north). Cells are space-separated (one symbol = one cell).
GRID REF — cell(col,row) centre = [lng, lat]:  lng = -73.9899926 + (col+0.5)*2.2933e-5;  lat = 40.7606717 - (row+0.5)*3.4868e-5
CROSS-REFERENCE: the layers share coordinates. Look up the SAME (col,row) in both layers: an equipment marker over '#' in LAYER 1 sits INSIDE that building; a cable glyph over '#' crosses that building — UNLESS that building is the cable's own source or target (a drop legitimately ENDS inside the building it serves; check CABLES source -> target).
HOW TO READ (hypothetical example, NOT from this scene): if LAYER 2 shows marker 'q' at (3,1) and LAYER 1 at (3,1) shows '#', that equipment sits INSIDE that building; '=' or '|' there means it sits on a street; ':' or '.' means open ground. The LEGEND's inside= field precomputes building containment exactly for every equipment item.
GEOMETRY vs TOPOLOGY: the grids show WHERE things are (geometry). WHO connects to whom (topology) is in the LEGEND — each closure's serves= list and the CABLES section's source -> target. For connectivity/path questions, read the LEGEND; do not trace glyphs.
THRESHOLDS: when a question states a numeric distance (e.g. 'within ~8m'), compare it against the LEGEND's measurements (d_street=, x/y meters) — grid glyphs are drawings, not distance judgments.

TEXT MAP v2 — two ALIGNED layers, one grid frame, north-up. 1 cell ≈ 3.0m. col 0..47 = W→E, row 0..27 = N→S (row 0 = north). Cells are space-separated (one symbol = one cell).
GRID REF — cell(col,row) centre = [lng, lat]:  lng = -73.9875318 + (col+0.5)*2.8146e-5;  lat = 40.7477417 - (row+0.5)*3.3094e-5
CROSS-REFERENCE: the layers share coordinates. Look up the SAME (col,row) in both layers: an equipment marker over '#' in LAYER 1 sits INSIDE that building; a cable glyph over '#' crosses that building — UNLESS that building is the cable's own source or target (a drop legitimately ENDS inside the building it serves; check CABLES source -> target).
HOW TO READ (hypothetical example, NOT from this scene): if LAYER 2 shows marker 'q' at (3,1) and LAYER 1 at (3,1) shows '#', that equipment sits INSIDE that building; '=' or '|' there means it sits on a street; ':' or '.' means open ground. The LEGEND's inside= field precomputes building containment exactly for every equipment item.
GEOMETRY vs TOPOLOGY: the grids show WHERE things are (geometry). WHO connects to whom (topology) is in the LEGEND — each closure's serves= list and the CABLES section's source -> target. For connectivity/path questions, read the LEGEND; do not trace glyphs.
THRESHOLDS: when a question states a numeric distance (e.g. 'within ~8m'), compare it against the LEGEND's measurements (d_street=, x/y meters) — grid glyphs are drawings, not distance judgments.

TEXT MAP v2 — two ALIGNED layers, one grid frame, north-up. 1 cell ≈ 3.4m. col 0..47 = W→E, row 0..27 = N→S (row 0 = north). Cells are space-separated (one symbol = one cell).
GRID REF — cell(col,row) centre = [lng, lat]:  lng = -73.9806813 + (col+0.5)*3.5202e-5;  lat = 40.7498536 - (row+0.5)*3.4985e-5
CROSS-REFERENCE: the layers share coordinates. Look up the SAME (col,row) in both layers: an equipment marker over '#' in LAYER 1 sits INSIDE that building; a cable glyph over '#' crosses that building — UNLESS that building is the cable's own source or target (a drop legitimately ENDS inside the building it serves; check CABLES source -> target).
HOW TO READ (hypothetical example, NOT from this scene): if LAYER 2 shows marker 'q' at (3,1) and LAYER 1 at (3,1) shows '#', that equipment sits INSIDE that building; '=' or '|' there means it sits on a street; ':' or '.' means open ground. The LEGEND's inside= field precomputes building containment exactly for every equipment item.
GEOMETRY vs TOPOLOGY: the grids show WHERE things are (geometry). WHO connects to whom (topology) is in the LEGEND — each closure's serves= list and the CABLES section's source -> target. For connectivity/path questions, read the LEGEND; do not trace glyphs.
THRESHOLDS: when a question states a numeric distance (e.g. 'within ~8m'), compare it against the LEGEND's measurements (d_street=, x/y meters) — grid glyphs are drawings, not distance judgments.

TEXT MAP v2 — two ALIGNED layers, one grid frame, north-up. 1 cell ≈ 3.8m. col 0..47 = W→E, row 0..27 = N→S (row 0 = north). Cells are space-separated (one symbol = one cell).
GRID REF — cell(col,row) centre = [lng, lat]:  lng = -73.9867091 + (col+0.5)*3.0229e-5;  lat = 40.7481646 - (row+0.5)*4.4693e-5
CROSS-REFERENCE: the layers share coordinates. Look up the SAME (col,row) in both layers: an equipment marker over '#' in LAYER 1 sits INSIDE that building; a cable glyph over '#' crosses that building — UNLESS that building is the cable's own source or target (a drop legitimately ENDS inside the building it serves; check CABLES source -> target).
HOW TO READ (hypothetical example, NOT from this scene): if LAYER 2 shows marker 'q' at (3,1) and LAYER 1 at (3,1) shows '#', that equipment sits INSIDE that building; '=' or '|' there means it sits on a street; ':' or '.' means open ground. The LEGEND's inside= field precomputes building containment exactly for every equipment item.
GEOMETRY vs TOPOLOGY: the grids show WHERE things are (geometry). WHO connects to whom (topology) is in the LEGEND — each closure's serves= list and the CABLES section's source -> target. For connectivity/path questions, read the LEGEND; do not trace glyphs.
THRESHOLDS: when a question states a numeric distance (e.g. 'within ~8m'), compare it against the LEGEND's measurements (d_street=, x/y meters) — grid glyphs are drawings, not distance judgments.
